column1: # Carbon Fiber Reinforced Polymer Laser Cleaning Dataset
# Source: Z-Beam Laser Cleaning Research Lab

# Citation: Z-Beam (2025). Carbon Fiber Reinforced Polymer Laser Cleaning Dataset. Retrieved from http://localhost:3000/materials/composite/fiber-reinforced/carbon-fiber-reinforced-polymer-laser-cleaning

column9: Optimal average power for CFRP cleaning without matrix damage
Near-IR wavelength for optimal carbon absorption and minimal polymer damage
Focused spot size for precise cleaning resolution
High repetition rate for efficient cleaning with thermal management
Fluence threshold for effective contaminant removal without CFRP damage
Nanosecond pulse duration for controlled ablation of contaminants
Optimal scanning speed for uniform cleaning coverage
Recommended number of passes for complete contaminant removal
Optimal beam overlap for uniform cleaning without excessive heating

Typical range for pulsed Nd:YAG lasers in CFRP cleaning to ablate contaminants without matrix degradation; starting at 30W for light contamination. Max safe ~100W to avoid thermal damage (thermal conductivity ~1-5 W/mK, low melting point of epoxy ~150-200°C). Source: Journal of Laser Applications (2018) on composite cleaning.

Effective range balances cleaning efficiency and heat input; recommended 1500 mm/s for optimal CFRP surface prep. Slower (500 mm/s) for heavy contamination, faster (3000 mm/s) for light. Source: Industry manuals from IPG Photonics and Laserax equipment specs for polymer composites.

Nanosecond regime preferred for CFRP to minimize heat-affected zone (HAZ <50 μm); picosecond (ps) or femtosecond for ultra-precise but costlier. 20 ns typical for 1064 nm ablation of surface contaminants without fiber delamination. Source: Laser physics studies in Optics and Lasers in Engineering (2020).

Standard for industrial cleaning; 30 kHz recommended for CFRP to achieve fast processing while limiting heat accumulation in low-conductivity matrix. Higher rates increase speed but risk overheating. Source: Academic literature in Applied Surface Science (2019) on pulsed laser cleaning of composites.

Fluence range for contaminant removal (e.g., oils, paints); CFRP damage threshold ~2-5 J/cm² depending on matrix. Recommended 1.5 J/cm² for safe cleaning, below ablation threshold for carbon fibers (~10 J/cm²). Conservative to account for reflectivity (~30-50% at 1064 nm). Source: Ablation threshold studies in Composites Part B (2017).

Beam diameter range for precision vs. area coverage; 200 μm recommended for CFRP to focus energy on contaminants without wide HAZ. Smaller for fine details, larger for broad surfaces. Source: Equipment specifications from Coherent and Trumpf laser cleaning systems for composites.

Single pass for light contamination, multi-pass for heavy; 2 passes recommended for complete CFRP cleaning with minimal damage risk. Multi-pass allows lower fluence per pass. Source: Industry case studies in Journal of Cleaner Production (2021) on laser surface preparation of polymers.

Ensures uniform coverage without excessive overlap heating; 60% recommended for CFRP to achieve even cleaning while controlling thermal load. Typical 30-80% in optimization. Source: Process studies in International Journal of Advanced Manufacturing Technology (2022).
